_comment: Per-chart narratives. CI (build_prompt.py -> GitHub Models -> write_narrative.py) overwrites these from live numbers; this committed copy is the fallback so the page always reads well. source = 'fallback' | 'github-models'.

notes.fossil: Current prices show WTI crude at approximately $79.2 per barrel, Brent crude at $81.62 per barrel, and US regular gasoline at $3.85 per gallon as of June 2026. Henry Hub natural gas is priced at $2.83 per MMBtu. Natural gas has exhibited the greatest volatility compared to crude oil and gasoline when indexed to 2019 levels, spiking sharply in 2022 before retreating. Meanwhile, crude oil and gasoline prices have tended to move more closely in tandem during this period.
notes.transition: The levelised cost of electricity, which represents the average total cost to build and operate a power plant over its lifetime divided by the total energy output, currently benchmarks solar PV at $44/MWh and onshore wind at $33/MWh globally. These costs are now lower than those of new coal plants at $110/MWh and combined cycle gas turbine (CCGT) plants at $76/MWh. Notably, the cost of solar PV has decreased by about 90% from roughly $460/MWh in 2010 to $44/MWh today, significantly improving its economic competitiveness in power generation.
notes.currency: An estimated 20% of global oil trade is settled outside the US dollar, reflecting a gradual shift though the dollar remains the dominant currency for oil transactions. This estimated figure, lacking official data, is influenced by Russia's strategic pivot towards Asian buyers and the increasing use of yuan and rupee for oil payments. Despite this trend, the US dollar continues to play the central role in pricing and settling most of the global oil market.
notes.now: Current energy prices and costs demonstrate a complex interplay between market volatility, technological advancements, and geopolitical shifts. While fossil fuel prices show varied stability and volatility, renewable energy sources continue to reduce costs substantially. Simultaneously, currency patterns in oil trading reveal a slow but noticeable diversification, highlighting evolving market dynamics without altering the dominant role of the US dollar.
